critic_raw:
The methodology is carefully designed and the authors show genuine awareness of many common failure modes (temporal leakage, cluster adjustment, preprocessing fit-on-train-only, one-time test set use). That said, I find three issues that are genuine rather than manufactured.

**Issue 1: The label construction rule creates systematic leakage via the 72-hour confirmation window**

The specific claim: An alert is positive if a confirmed incident disposition appears within 72 hours of alert creation. All preprocessing and model selection is done on this label.

The mechanism of failure: The 72-hour window spans across the training/validation/test boundary for alerts that fall in the final 72 hours of each window. An alert at the last day of month 12 (end of training) whose confirmation ticket is created at hour 60 falls in month 13 (start of validation). But more critically, the same analyst behavior that generates positive labels is itself a signal: whether an analyst 'opened a ticket' correlates with alert features like severity or rule family that are also model features. This is not classic future-label leakage but something subtler—the label itself is an analyst-effort proxy, and analyst effort is non-uniform across the 18-month window. If analyst capacity increased mid-period, the positive rate in training would be lower than in test, making the label distribution shift over time independent of true incident prevalence. The model would be trained on a stricter definition of 'confirmed' than it is evaluated on, or vice versa.

What constitutes evidence: Plot the raw confirmed-incident rate by month. If it is not roughly stationary (allowing for seasonal campaign variation), that is a sign that analyst capacity, not incident frequency, is driving label variation. A secondary check: compare the rate at which 'no ticket created' alerts from training-period months eventually receive tickets (as discovered by looking at later SIEM data) versus the declared negative rate. A large discrepancy implies systematic under-labeling in training.

**Issue 2: Cluster-adjusted MAP@20 is applied only at evaluation, but training pairs are formed at the day-batch level without cluster awareness—this creates a misalignment between training loss and evaluation metric**

The specific claim: Training pairs are formed within each day's alert batch (confirmed vs. benign). Evaluation uses cluster-adjusted MAP where multiple alerts from the same incident cluster count as one confirmed finding.

The mechanism of failure: LambdaMART/LightGBM ranking with pairwise loss will optimize for pushing *each individual confirmed alert* above benign alerts. But if an incident generates 15 nearly identical alerts (same rule ID, same source/dest, within 24 hours—exactly the cluster definition used in evaluation), the model is trained to push all 15 above benign alerts, and the pairwise loss counts 15 satisfied pairs. At evaluation, those 15 alerts collapsed to one confirmed finding contribute at most one precision hit. This means the model may achieve high pairwise training accuracy by learning to score noisy, high-volume alert floods very high, while MAP@20 only rewards the first hit from each cluster. In a busy queue, a single large campaign cluster could dominate the top-20 slots (all from the same incident) with MAP@20 seeing only one confirmed finding, making the model's apparent strength (it learned to rank campaign alerts high) look like a weakness in the metric. Alternatively, the model achieves high MAP@20 on days with exactly one cluster and fails on days with cluster-heavy traffic. Neither of these is caught by averaging MAP@20 across days without stratifying by queue cluster density.

What constitutes evidence: Report MAP@20 separately for days where the top-20 queue contains alerts from ≥3 distinct incident clusters versus days where ≥50% of the top-20 slots are occupied by a single cluster. If performance diverges substantially, the training objective and evaluation metric are misaligned. An alternative is to train with cluster-aware lambdas (weight the pairwise contribution of within-cluster pairs downward), which would close this gap.

**Issue 3: The DistilBERT embeddings are pre-trained on general-domain text; the prerequisite for the text-signal hypothesis to operate is that alert descriptions contain semantically discriminative natural-language content that a general-domain embedding can distinguish—this is unverified**

The specific claim: Dense text embeddings (384-dim DistilBERT) of alert descriptions add signal beyond metadata. The comparison is learning-to-rank with embeddings vs. logistic regression without.

The mechanism of failure: SIEM alert descriptions are often templated strings: 'High severity alert: rule XYZ triggered on host ABC.' The semantic variation between description texts may be almost entirely captured by rule ID and severity—features already in the metadata baseline. If so, the embedding is a noisy, high-dimensional encoding of the same information the baseline already has, and any improvement attributed to 'text' is actually marginal. More problematically, if alert description templates correlate with specific historical incident clusters (because rule XYZ was the rule associated with a confirmed campaign in months 3–7), the embedding will pick up a spurious temporal correlation—the model learns that embeddings near the centroid of rule-XYZ descriptions predict incidents, which is just a proxy for rule ID already in the metadata. This would inflate the advantage of Feature Set A over Feature Set B without reflecting a genuine text-understanding benefit.

A subtler failure: the secondary analysis proposed (train a baseline classifier on Feature Set A) would catch whether ranking vs. text is the driver, but it would NOT catch whether the text embedding adds real semantic signal vs. merely acting as a soft cluster ID. If the embedding improvement disappears when rule ID is target-encoded with enough cardinality, the text hypothesis is not supported.

What constitutes evidence: Compute the average cosine similarity between alert description embeddings that share the same rule ID versus those that do not. If within-rule-ID embedding similarity is very high (>0.95), the embeddings are near-redundant with rule ID. Also run an ablation: Feature Set A minus the text embedding (i.e., just metadata with the ranking formulation) vs. Feature Set B (metadata with classification). This isolates the ranking formulation contribution cleanly and is currently missing from the design—the proposed secondary analysis (classifier on Feature Set A) does not produce this ablation.

**Summary:** Issues 1 and 3 are potentially resolvable by additional analysis within the existing design. Issue 2 is a structural misalignment between training objective and evaluation metric that could cause the primary metric to mismeasure the model's true operational behavior. None of these are fatal to the study design, but Issue 2 in particular could cause the model to be selected or rejected on misleading grounds.
all_issues_raised: The 72-hour confirmation window used for label construction may cause analyst-capacity-driven label distribution shift across the temporal split, conflating incident prevalence with analyst throughput changes.

Training pairs are formed at the individual-alert level within daily batches, while MAP@20 applies cluster-level deduplication at evaluation, creating a misalignment between the training loss and the evaluation metric that may reward or penalize the model for the wrong behavior.

Pre-trained general-domain DistilBERT embeddings applied to templated SIEM alert descriptions may encode near-redundant information already captured by rule ID, and the proposed secondary analysis does not include the ablation (ranking formulation with metadata only, no text) needed to isolate these contributions.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 72-hour confirmation window used for label construction may cause analyst-capacity-driven label distribution shift across the temporal split, conflating incident prevalence with analyst throughput changes.

Training pairs are formed at the individual-alert level within daily batches, while MAP@20 applies cluster-level deduplication at evaluation, creating a misalignment between the training loss and the evaluation metric that may reward or penalize the model for the wrong behavior.

Pre-trained general-domain DistilBERT embeddings applied to templated SIEM alert descriptions may encode near-redundant information already captured by rule ID, and the proposed secondary analysis does not include the ablation (ranking formulation with metadata only, no text) needed to isolate these contributions.
